sources.citation: Stanford HAI. AI Definitions and Explainers.
Turing. Computing Machinery and Intelligence. Mind, 1950.

learning_objectives: L1: Define artificial intelligence and distinguish narrow AI from general AI.
L1: Identify the four AIMA definitions of AI (thinking/acting humanly/rationally).
L1: Name key milestones: Turing test 1950, Dartmouth 1956, AlexNet 2012, Transformer 2017.
L2: Describe supervised, unsupervised, and reinforcement learning conceptually.
L2: Explain the Turing Test and its limitations as a measure of intelligence.
L2: Compare rule-based AI with machine learning approaches.
L3: Classify a real-world AI application by learning type, problem domain, and societal impact.
L3: Evaluate whether a system meets criteria for narrow vs general AI.

concepts.title: Defining AI: Four Perspectives
concepts.explanation: Russell & Norvig organise AI into 2×2: thinking vs acting, human vs rational standard. Modern AI focuses on rational agents — perceive via sensors, act via actuators to maximise a performance measure. Narrow AI: task-specific. AGI: human-level flexibility — does not exist as of 2026.
concepts.worked_example: AlphaFold→rational acting. GPT→acting humanly. Deep Blue→rational acting via alpha-beta search.

concepts.title: The Turing Test and Its Limitations
concepts.explanation: Turing (1950): if interrogator cannot distinguish machine from human via text, machine is 'intelligent'. Limitations: anthropocentric, gameable (ELIZA), excludes embodiment, Chinese Room argument (Searle 1980) — syntax ≠ semantics.
concepts.worked_example: ELIZA (1966): substitution rules only, no internal model — yet users believed it understood them.

concepts.title: Key Milestones
concepts.explanation: 1943 McCulloch-Pitts neuron. 1950 Turing paper. 1956 Dartmouth — 'AI' coined. 1966 ELIZA. 1980s expert systems. 1987 Second AI Winter. 1997 Deep Blue. 2012 AlexNet. 2017 Transformer. 2020 AlphaFold. 2022+ LLMs.
concepts.worked_example: Two AI Winters: promises outpaced results. Each revival needed new hardware (GPUs), data (internet scale), and methods (deep learning).

concepts.title: Why Expert Systems Failed
concepts.explanation: Hand-crafted rules: knowledge acquisition bottleneck, brittleness outside rule base, cannot learn. ML overcame these by learning from data.
concepts.worked_example: MYCIN: 65–70% diagnostic accuracy, never deployed due to liability, brittleness, and integration barriers.

concepts.title: Supervised, Unsupervised, Reinforcement Learning
concepts.explanation: Supervised: learn f(X)→y from labelled examples. Unsupervised: find patterns in unlabelled data. Reinforcement: agent maximises cumulative reward via trial and error. Self-supervised: model creates its own labels (e.g. next-token prediction).
concepts.worked_example: Spam→supervised. Customer segments→unsupervised. AlphaGo→RL. GPT pre-training→self-supervised.

concepts.title: PEAS Framework
concepts.explanation: Rational agent: selects actions maximising performance measure given percept history. PEAS: Performance, Environment, Actuators, Sensors. Environment properties: fully/partially observable, deterministic/stochastic, episodic/sequential, discrete/continuous, single/multi-agent.
concepts.worked_example: Self-driving car PEAS — P: safe/fast/legal; E: roads/traffic/weather; A: steering/throttle/brake; S: cameras/LIDAR/GPS/radar.

concepts.title: Four Agent Types
concepts.explanation: Simple reflex: condition-action rules. Model-based: adds internal state. Goal-based: searches action sequences. Utility-based: maximises expected utility. Learning agents: improve over time.
concepts.worked_example: Thermostat→simple reflex. GPS navigator→goal-based. Self-driving car→utility-based+learning.
